reports.title: Ares Management Corp. (ARES): ARCC read-across: Slower deployment and modest pick up in non-accruals
reports.headline: GS says ARCC's 1Q26 print points to slower direct-lending deployment, muted 2Q activity, and modestly worse credit metrics, with implications for ARES fees and sentiment.
reports.summary: Goldman reiterates Buy on ARES and uses ARCC's 1Q26 results as a read-across for ARES' credit business: net deployment slowed sharply, April activity stayed muted, and non-accruals ticked up modestly, reinforcing a softer NT origination backdrop. The note argues scaled platforms like ARES should benefit when activity normalizes given roughly $100bn of available credit capital, but investors are likely to stay focused on whether weaker deployment and rising non-accruals signal broader credit-performance divergence across managers.
important_observations: Net new commitments fell sharply even though new-money spreads stayed broadly stable, implying the bottleneck is activity/vol rather than pricing alone.
April commitment pace remained soft and backlog declined, suggesting 2Q deployment may stay muted absent a market reopening.
Non-accruals increased but remain within historical ranges, keeping focus on whether underlying credit trends are starting to bifurcate across managers.
Portfolio EBITDA weakened q/q while leverage edged up, a combination that bears watching if private-credit fundamentals soften further.
ARCC management fees were modestly ahead of GS's ARES model, providing a small offset to softer deployment trends.
new_data: 1Q26 gross new commitments were $3,246mn vs $5,825mn in 4Q25 and $3,453mn y/y.
1Q26 net new commitments slowed to $70mn vs $1,164mn in 4Q25 and $596mn y/y.
1Q26 net fundings were $277mn vs $515mn in 4Q25 and $243mn y/y.
Debt-investment yield on fundings was 9.2% in 1Q26 vs 9.9% in 1Q25; implied spread was ~550bps on quarter-end SOFR.
Apr MTD through Apr 23 gross commitments were ~$198mn vs exits/repayments of ~$13mn; backlog was ~$1.8bn vs $2.2bn at end-Jan.
Non-accruals rose 30bps q/q to 2.1% of cost; fair-value non-accruals were 1.2%, flat q/q.
Portfolio weighted-average EBITDA fell 5% q/q to $291mn; net lev rose to 5.7x from 5.6x.
Mgmt fees were $111mn, ~3% above GS's published ARES model and +9% y/y.
management_insights: Mgmt flagged muted activity so far in 2Q, with commitments below prior-year April levels.
ARCC disclosed only sub-$200mn of commitments through Apr 23, underscoring a slower origination environment.
GS expects ARES can accelerate deployment once markets stabilize because of its scale and ~$100bn of available capital.
Credit performance is likely to stay in focus as non-accrual trends begin to diverge by manager.

estimate_change_details.note: PT cut per GS target-price history table; Buy rating reiterated.

evidence_quality: Primary: based mainly on ARCC's company-reported 1Q26 results, earnings release, slide deck, and 10-Q datapoints.
Primary: includes company-disclosed Apr MTD origination/backlog metrics, which are timely and directly relevant for NT read-across.
Triangulated but indirect: conclusions for ARES rely on ARCC as a fee/contribution proxy rather than direct ARES segment reporting.
Mostly analyst inference for broader alt-manager implications, especially around deployment normalization and manager-by-manager credit divergence.
risks_or_watch_items: Wider credit spreads or ongoing vol could keep deployment and fee growth muted.
Further increases in non-accruals could pressure sentiment and raise concern on asset quality.
Backlog erosion and weak Apr commitments raise risk that 2Q originations stay below recent run-rate.
Realization timing remains a downside risk per GS.
Watch whether portfolio EBITDA pressure and slightly higher lev become a broader trend.
key_numbers: ARES rating/PT: Buy, $131 PT; price $112.18; implied upside 16.8%.
ARCC contributes 30%+ of ARES Credit segment mgmt fees incl. Part I fees.
1Q26 gross commitments: $3,246mn; net new commitments: $70mn; net fundings: $277mn.
Apr 23 MTD gross commitments: ~$198mn; exits/repayments: ~$13mn; backlog: ~$1.8bn.
Yield on funded debt investments: 9.2% in 1Q26; Apr funded yield: 9.1%; exited/repaid yield: 9.7%.
Non-accruals: 2.1% of cost vs 1.8% in 4Q25; 1.2% of fair value.
Interest coverage: 2.2x; weighted-average portfolio EBITDA: $291mn; net lev: 5.7x.
Mgmt fees: $111mn; Part I fees: $84mn vs Visible Alpha cons $88mn.

reports.title: CQ1'16 Earnings Preview: ANET, INFN, SNDK, STX, WDC Plus HDD / SSD Forecast Update and Raising STX PT to $27
reports.headline: Citi previews CQ1/FQ3 storage and networking prints, cuts LT HDD unit ests, lifts SSD outlook, trims WDC FY17/FY18 EPS, and raises STX PT to $27.
reports.summary: Citi’s 7-Apr-2016 preview is a multi-name storage/networking note with the key sector call being a lower 2017-2018 HDD unit outlook and higher SSD adoption assumptions. For WDC, Citi sees limited downside into FQ3'16 with rev/EPS roughly in line, but flows the weaker HDD / faster SSD mix into its model and trims FY17/FY18 EPS to $6.32/$6.66 from $6.40/$6.95. Citi still prefers WDC over STX on better positioning for SSD migration and the pending SNDK deal, while separately raising STX’s PT to $27 on multiple expansion despite maintaining a Sell.
important_observations: Citi’s main variant view is that the HDD decline extends into 2017-2018 as soft PC demand and faster SSD migration pressure the storage complex more than prior assumptions.
For WDC, downside into the quarter is framed as limited because Citi is using a conservative ~100M drive TAM vs industry at 103-104M, reducing the risk of a material near-term miss.
Citi remains structurally more positive on WDC than STX because WDC is viewed as better levered to SSD migration and captive NAND via the pending SNDK acquisition.
Enterprise storage mix is shifting faster toward SSDs, which Citi argues strengthens the strategic logic of the WDC-SNDK merger.
ANET’s 2H setup is tied to Tomahawk-based upgrades and 100Gb spine buildouts, while INFN’s thesis leans on Metro/DCI growth offset by telco capex seasonality and R&D drag.
SNDK fundamentals appear less important than deal timing, with the stock viewed as trading mainly on the pending WDC acquisition and remaining MOFCOM approval.
new_data: HDD unit ests cut to -5.4% growth in 2017 and -4.0% in 2018 from -0.3% and -1.8%; 2016 HDD decline unchanged at -12%.
Client SSD attach implied at 55% of total PC shipments exiting 2018 vs 22% currently.
Total HDD units revised to 392.7M in 2017 from 414.0M and 377.1M in 2018 from 406.5M.
Enterprise HDD units revised to 63.5M in 2017 from 68.2M and 61.8M in 2018 from 68.2M.
WDC FQ3'16 preview: rev $2.856B, EPS $1.28, 42.1M HDD units, ASP $62.
STX FQ3'16 preview: rev $2.696B, EPS $0.60, 40.8M HDD units, ASP $60.
SNDK CQ1'16 preview: rev $1.231B, EPS $0.57, with bits shipped down 10% q/q and ASPs down 8% q/q.
management_insights: SNDK is expected to issue results and written commentary without a conference call as the WDC merger approaches close.
Citi expects WDC to guide FQ4'16 toward a slightly >100M HDD TAM and seasonal rev recovery to $2.9B-$3.0B.
Citi expects ANET commentary to stay constructive on Webscale upgrades and 100Gb spine deployments into 2H16.

estimate_change_details.note: Citi trimmed WDC FY17 EPS on updated HDD/SSD industry assumptions.
Citi trimmed WDC FY18 EPS on updated HDD/SSD industry assumptions.
PT raised on sector multiple expansion and more balanced NT risk.
Citi lowered FY17 EPS to reflect HDD/SSD model updates.
Citi lowered FY18 EPS to reflect HDD/SSD model updates.

evidence_quality: Primary/analyst model work: detailed Citi earnings previews, company guidance ranges, and Citi vs Street bridges for ANET/INFN/SNDK/STX/WDC.
Market/industry data: HDD and SSD unit forecasts appear built from Trendfocus, FactSet, and Citi’s storage model; useful for sector framing but still model-driven.
Triangulated channel/adjacent data: note cites Webscale capex, NA telco capex, supplier commentary, and Taiwan semi supply-chain read-throughs rather than new company-reported datapoints.
Limited primary mgmt evidence: this is largely a pre-earnings inference note, not a post-call readout or mgmt mtg summary, so conviction on quarter specifics should be weighted accordingly.
risks_or_watch_items: PC demand could stay weaker for longer, pressuring HDD TAM and WDC/STX estimates further.
Faster-than-modeled SSD migration remains the key structural risk to HDD-centric names.
WDC-SNDK close timing still depends on final MOFCOM approval.
STX may see NT GM upside, but Citi still sees LT strategy and possible disruptive M&A as overhangs.
WDC’s near-term print is sensitive to HDD units/ASP around a ~100M industry TAM assumption.
INFN demand outlook still depends on telco capex seasonality and whether Metro/DCI offsets hold.
key_numbers: WDC FQ3'16 est: rev $2.856B, EPS $1.28, GM 28.8%, OM 11.3%, HDD units 42.1M, ASP $62.
WDC FY17/FY18 EPS revised to $6.32/$6.66 from $6.40/$6.95.
STX PT raised to $27 from $23; FY17/FY18 EPS cut to $3.56/$3.38 from $3.62/$3.46.

reports.title: Seagate Technology PLC: The Dawn of The Zettabyte Age
reports.headline: BTIG argues STX is underappreciated as a core beneficiary of secular data growth, with HDD remaining the economic capacity tier for cloud and edge-generated data; Maintain Buy, $55 PT.
reports.summary: BTIG reiterates Buy on STX and frames the stock as a mispriced beneficiary of the coming "zettabyte age," arguing that secular data creation, cloud build-outs, AI/ML, and edge computing should sustain HDD bit demand well beyond what unit declines imply. The note’s core thesis is that while flash dominates performance use cases, HDD remains the only economical medium for large-scale capacity storage, supporting STX’s mix shift toward higher-margin nearline enterprise and improved EPS power.
important_observations: Core debate is not whether data grows, but whether investors are underestimating the value of the low-cost storage layer required to retain that data at scale.
BTIG argues counting HDD units misses the thesis; bit/EB growth and mix shift to high-capacity enterprise drives are more relevant than spindle declines.
Nearline enterprise is already becoming the economic engine of STX, with materially better growth, mix, and profit profile than legacy PC/client HDD.

The note frames STX as a variant-perception idea: bearish HDD views are inconsistent with bullish secular data-growth views unless memory capex rises massively or data-retention assumptions prove too optimistic.
BTIG sees cloud and intelligent-edge buildouts as additive, not cannibalistic, with edge inference increasing the need for centralized cloud storage for training, retention, and metadata.
Valuation argument is that the market is still pricing STX on cyclical/legacy HDD concerns rather than as a secular data infrastructure beneficiary.
new_data: IDC Data Age 2025 forecast: global datasphere to reach 163 ZB by 2025 vs 16.1 ZB in 2016.
IDC forecast: hypercritical data to grow at 54% CAGR vs 30% for total data.
IDC forecast: embedded data to reach 20% of all data created by 2020.
IDC forecast: IoT data to represent 95% of real-time data by 2025.
IDC forecast: analyzed data to reach 5.2 ZB by 2024; data interacting with cognitive systems to reach 1.4 ZB by 2025.
BTIG cloud capex forecast for major platforms rises to $66.7B in FY18E from $50.9B in FY16A.
BTIG shows flash remains at a large $/GB premium to enterprise HDD through FY25E despite narrowing.
IDC/BTIG suggest HDD still represents roughly 50% of storage bits shipped in 2025, with enterprise HDD >80% of enterprise HDD/SSD mix.
BTIG base-case storage model assumes 18% combined data growth and ~1.8 ZB HDD demand by 2025.
management_insights: Mgmt has publicly discussed a $30B-$40B HDD opportunity based on 1.5-2.0 ZB of HDD storage demand.
Company guidance for CY17 EPS of at least $4.50 was well above prior market expectations and used by BTIG as evidence that the mix/cost reset is working.
BTIG cites public signs of healthier factory utilization, stable pricing, post-flood-high GM, and continued mix shift away from PC exposure.
Mgmt appears focused on portfolio realignment toward high-capacity cloud/enterprise, surveillance, gaming, DVR, and other higher-capacity use cases.
estimate_changes: No explicit analyst rev/EPS/PT revision versus BTIG’s prior note is disclosed; BTIG maintains Buy and $55 PT.
The note highlights stronger company outlook vs prior market expectations, especially CY17 EPS guide of at least $4.50 vs prior consensus near $3.80.
evidence_quality: Primary/triangulated: uses IDC's March 2017 Data Age 2025 study, company-reported results/guidance, and BTIG/FactSet market data.
Primary company evidence: references Seagate-reported nearline EB growth, rev mix, margin trends, and company outlook.
Mostly analyst model work/inference: long-dated HDD/NAND bit-growth, revenue, and EPS power scenarios are BTIG framework assumptions rather than company guidance.
Some third-party industry support: Gartner, Google white paper, silicon supply commentary, and cloud capex datasets help triangulate storage-demand and supply constraints.
Weight should be moderate: strong for secular framing and current mix/profit trends, lower for precise 2020-2025 TAM, bit-share, and valuation outcomes.
risks_or_watch_items: CSP demand remains lumpy with limited NT visibility despite broader customer diversification.
If NAND cost declines or memory capex ramp faster than BTIG assumes, HDD economics and long-term share could weaken.
Retention assumptions matter: if more real-time/IoT data is discarded rather than stored, capacity demand could underwhelm.
Legacy PC/client HDD declines still pressure consolidated units and can obscure improvement in higher-value segments.
Execution risk remains around sustaining double-digit capacity growth, stable pricing, and continued enterprise mix gains.
key_numbers: PT methodology: 12x run-rate EPS of about $4.50.
Seagate high-capacity enterprise EB growth: 63% in CY16.
High-capacity enterprise rev: about $2.7B in CY16; rev mix expanded from 21% to 32%.

reports.title: Moving STX to Top Pick As We Gain Greater Confidence In Cycle Lonity and Margins
reports.headline: MS moves STX to Top Pick, keeps OW, cuts PT to $129 from $133; thesis is cycle longevity + structurally higher GM despite a transitory Mar-qtr supply hit.
reports.summary: MS upgrades STX to its IT Hardware Top Pick on rising conviction that the HDD upcycle has more room to run, Mar-qtr supply issues are temporary, and Street GM/EPS remain too low as HAMR mix scales. The note cuts PT to $129 from $133 on lower NTM EPS, but argues for a June-qtr rev snap-back, continued pricing strength, tighter supply, and FY26-27 EPS upside vs cons driven by materially higher GM assumptions.
important_observations: June-qtr rev snap-back is central to the call: MS sees +11% q/q rev growth vs typical -1% seasonality once supply constraints ease.
Variant view is on margins, not just demand: MS sees FY26-27 GM at 37.8%/39.6%, well above Street, driven by HAMR mix and stronger non-HAMR pricing/profitability.
Recent industry checks suggest NL HDD demand remains robust after 6 qtrs of capacity growth, with CSP inventories still normal and lead times still ~52 weeks.
MS argues the market is overly focused on cyclical rollover/share loss risk while underappreciating STX's 24/28TB traction and HAMR-led competitiveness into C2H25/CY26.
High short interest and low expectations could amplify upside if post-earnings confidence builds around June recovery, further HAMR quals, and FY26 buybacks.

CSP HDD inventories are described as normal/not elevated.

Asia component-supplier checks suggest better-than-seasonal Mar-qtr restocking tied to end-demand, not channel inventory build.
Updated HAMR volume/margin work now assumes HAMR is ~1% of HDD EB in FY25 and up to ~50% by FY27.

reports.title: Harnessing the Power of Heat: HDD Versus SSD - Ahead of the Curve Series
reports.headline: TD Cowen argues HAMR should extend HDD relevance in DC storage, with STX the clearest beneficiary and PT raised to $78.
reports.summary: TD Cowen lays out a bullish HDD/NL storage thesis centered on HAMR, arguing HDDs should remain the dominant DC storage medium through 2032 despite SSD gains because HAMR materially lowers HDD cost/TB and can lift OEM GMs. The report sees STX as the main beneficiary given its HAMR lead, models HDDs at ~80% of DC storage by decade-end, and views SSD adoption as constrained by cost, capex intensity, and high-capacity supply even during NAND downcycles.
important_observations: Variant view vs cons is that HDDs and SSDs remain complementary in DCs, with HDDs still storing ~80% of data by 2032.
HAMR is framed as a structural cost/margin inflection for HDDs, not just a capacity upgrade; 32TB HAMR is modeled at 30% lower cost/TB vs 20TB CMR.
AI is viewed as a net HDD demand tailwind because training datasets require cheap bulk storage, even if SSDs serve performance-sensitive workloads.
TD Cowen argues SSD share gains in NAND downcycles should be limited because hyperscalers treat trough SSD pricing as unsustainable and high-capacity SSD supply is constrained.
WDC is seen as behind STX on HAMR but not facing catastrophic share loss; Cowen expects only low/mid-single-digit share loss as WDC bridges with CMR/SMR scaling.
PSTG is viewed as a targeted beneficiary of lower NAND pricing and QLC adoption, but not a full nearline HDD replacement story.
LRCX is a read-through on rising NAND process complexity, especially as stacking/layer counts increase and deposition requirements rise.
new_data: Proprietary framework suggests HAMR can be up to 500 bps GM accretive for HDD OEMs.
Cowen estimates HAMR yields need to exceed ~40% to be GM-neutral vs CMR.
Field work indicates a major CSP could test ~25K HAMR drives in CY23 and potentially deploy up to 2M drives within 2 years if qualified.
Cowen models HDD cost/TB remaining ~4x cheaper than SSD by decade-end, or ~2x cheaper on TCO basis.
Cowen forecasts a $24B total HDD market by 2032, with NL HDD rev growing 7% CAGR from 2022.
Cowen estimates $1.5B of NAND WFE spend is needed for SSD suppliers to take 1% share from nearline HDDs.
management_insights: Seagate had shipped 32TB HAMR drives to a cloud customer for qualification in Apr-23, with rev ramp expected in early CY24.
Seagate CFO commentary cited by Cowen suggests HAMR product introductions every 18-20 months vs 10-12 months historically for PMR.
Mgmt LT GM target for STX is referenced at 30-33%; Cowen sees upside to ~34% by CY26 if HAMR ramps well.
Cowen notes WDC has improved enterprise SSD qualification execution after prior controller/firmware issues.

estimate_change_details.note: STX PT raised to $78 from $70.
Cowen frames HAMR upside as roughly $8.0/sh in earnings power; no prior EPS estimate disclosed.
Cowen models GM expansion toward 34%; prior published estimate revision not provided.

evidence_quality: Primary + analyst model work: report uses company-reported market data, disclosed product timing, and Cowen financial modeling.
Triangulated field work: multiple conclusions rely on channel checks/field work with CSPs and storage ecosystem contacts rather than solely public disclosures.
Proprietary dataset/inference: HAMR yield, GM accretion, cost/TB, and share scenarios are largely Cowen analytical constructs, not company-guided outputs.
Some datapoints are anecdotal but directionally useful, especially CSP qualification/deployment commentary on HAMR.
Weight should be moderate: thesis is detailed and cross-checked, but key conclusions depend on future HAMR yields, customer qualification, and NAND pricing behavior.
risks_or_watch_items: STX HAMR manufacturing yields and timing of full customer qualification.
Cloud/DC capex trends, since Cowen estimates ~70% of nearline HDD demand is tied to new DC builds.
Duration/severity of NAND pricing pressure and whether another sharp downcycle temporarily improves SSD competitiveness.
Ramp of 30TB+ high-capacity SSDs and whether supply broadens faster than expected.
Potential NAND technology improvements that re-accelerate cost/TB declines.
Pressure in non-nearline HDD categories, where SSD substitution risk is higher.
key_numbers: HAMR could be up to 500 bps GM accretive for HDD OEMs.
HAMR yield threshold for GM neutrality estimated at ~40% vs ~85% CMR yields today.
HDDs seen at ~80% of DC storage by decade-end vs ~85-86% in 2022.
32TB HAMR modeled at 30% lower cost/TB vs 20TB CMR; 36TB HAMR ~10% lower vs 32TB HAMR.

reports.title: Seagate Technology | North America: Upgrading to Overweight On A More Attractive Multi-Year Earnings Story
reports.headline: MS upg STX to OW from EW and lifts PT to $115 on higher HAMR-driven GM upside and incremental AI-related HDD demand in FY26-27.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley upgrades STX to OW from EW and raises its PT to $115 from $73, arguing the stock is moving from a cyclical HDD recovery into a structurally stronger earnings phase. The key change is a more bullish bottom-up view on HAMR mix and margin accretion, with LT GM now seen at 32-35% vs mgmt's 30-33% target, plus modest AI-driven HDD exabyte upside in FY26-27. While HAMR mix lowers rev per exabyte and trims rev ests by 1-2%, MS sees the GM lift more than offsetting that pressure, driving 20-30% higher EPS ests and FY27 EPS power above $10.50.
important_observations: Core debate shifts from cyclical recovery timing to whether HAMR can structurally reset STX GM above prior through-cycle levels.
MS argues HAMR is materially margin accretive, with a modeled ~45% normalized GM for 30TB drives and further upside as 40TB/50TB products scale.
AI is framed less as an immediate rev driver and more as an outer-year storage tailwind that benefits HDD alongside flash rather than displacing it outright.
The note implies variant perception vs Street on profitability, with MS 200-300 bps ahead of Street GM in FY25/26 and >10% ahead of Street FY27 EPS power.
Despite lower price/TB and slightly lower rev, MS sees mix/technology leadership creating a stronger earnings algorithm through GM expansion and opex discipline.
Potential catalysts include HAMR customer quals starting as soon as May, broader US tier-1 CSP qualification by CY24-end, share gains, and a future Analyst Day that could lift LT GM guide.

reports.title: Harnessing the Power of Heat: HDD Versus SSD - Ahead of the Curve Series
reports.headline: TD Cowen argues HAMR keeps HDDs structurally advantaged vs eSSDs in DC storage, with STX the main equity beneficiary and PT raised to $78.
reports.summary: TD Cowen’s industry note argues HAMR is a step-change for nearline HDD economics, enabling materially lower $/TB and potentially higher OEM GMs, which should preserve HDD dominance in DC storage through 2032 despite SSD price declines. The core call is that HDDs remain the lowest-cost medium for the bulk of cold/warm data, SSD/HDD price parity is unlikely this decade if HAMR ramps, and STX is best positioned given its HAMR lead, while WDC should remain competitive with a lagged roadmap and PSTG/LRCX also benefit from separate NAND-related trends.
important_observations: Variant view vs cons is that HDDs and SSDs remain complementary, with ~80% of DC storage still on HDDs by 2032 rather than SSDs displacing HDDs before price parity.
HAMR is framed as a structural margin lever, not just a capacity upgrade, because higher TB/drive lowers BOM per TB and may improve pricing discipline in a consolidated HDD market.
AI is presented as net pos for HDD demand because training/inference ecosystems require large low-cost data lakes even if SSDs serve performance tiers.
SSD share gains in downturns are viewed as self-limiting because NAND capacity additions are highly capex intensive and hyperscalers design storage architectures well ahead of spot-price troughs.
WDC share loss risk is argued to be manageable because customer concentration, deliberate dual-sourcing, and WDC's interim roadmap could blunt STX's HAMR lead.
Non-nearline HDD remains the weak link; the constructive thesis is mainly about nearline/cloud rather than the full HDD portfolio.
new_data: Proprietary model suggests HAMR can be up to 500 bps accretive to HDD OEM GMs.
HAMR yields need to exceed ~40% to be GM-neutral vs current CMR economics.
32TB HAMR is modeled at ~30% lower cost/TB vs 20TB CMR; 36TB HAMR adds another ~10% cost/TB reduction vs 32TB HAMR.
TD Cowen sees HDD cost/TB staying ~4x cheaper than SSDs by decade-end, or ~2x cheaper on TCO after power/space/perf adjustments.
Nearline HDDs are projected to remain ~80% of DC storage by 2032, down from mid-80%s today.
Analyst field work indicates one major CSP could test ~25k HAMR units in CY23 and potentially deploy up to ~2M drives over 2 years if qualified.
management_insights: STX had shipped 32TB HAMR drives to a cloud customer for qualification in Apr-23, with rev ramp expected in early CY24.
STX mgmt's LT GM target is cited at 30-33%; TD Cowen believes HAMR could push STX toward ~34% GM by CY26.
STX CFO commentary indicates HAMR product introductions may occur every 18-20 months vs 10-12 months historically for PMR, implying more complex ramps.
WDC is described as executing well on ePMR/ultraSMR and improving enterprise SSD qualifications after prior controller/firmware issues.
PSTG is portrayed as leveraging DirectFlash/QLC capabilities and seeing selective cloud opportunities, but not as a full nearline HDD replacement across DC storage.

reports.title: 1Q26 First Look: Strong EPS Beat and Raise Reinforces ACA Recovery
reports.headline: CNC 1Q26 EPS beat by ~50% vs cons, driven by broad MLR outperformance; FY26 EPS guide raised by $0.40, supporting ACA recovery thesis.
reports.summary: Barclays frames CNC's 1Q26 print as a material beat-and-raise that should ease ACA margin concerns and reinforce the recovery case. Adj. EPS of $3.37 beat cons by ~48-50%, primarily on better-than-expected MLR across Commercial/ACA, Medicare, and Medicaid, while FY26 EPS guidance was lifted by $0.40 to >$3.40. Barclays highlights especially strong ACA and Medicare MLR performance, modestly better Medicaid enrollment, and commentary that pricing remains conservative despite higher-acuity silver-book utilization, leaving room for possible 2Q benefit from risk adjustment.
important_observations: ACA MLR beat was strong enough to absorb the specialty pharmacy headwind flagged in Mar, supporting the view that pricing conservatism remains embedded.
Broad-based MLR upside across all segments reduces concern that 1Q strength was isolated or timing-driven.
Mgmt said 1Q exceeded internal expectations by $0.50, implying the beat was not only vs Street numbers but also ahead of internal planning.
Updated guidance does not include any benefit from higher ACA acuity risk adjustment, creating a potential 2Q upside lever.
Medicaid margin performance benefited from both favorable seasonal flu dynamics and more durable drivers like rate increases and medical cost management.
Enrollment progression looked broadly consistent with prior guide after adjusting for the MDwise exit effect, lowering risk of a major membership disconnect.

ACA Exchange remains the central debate, with higher Silver-tier acuity and specialty Rx util creating uncertainty until 2026 risk adjustment benefits are realized.

Medicare margin progression continued, aided by lapping the 1Q25 PDP drag and better HBR.

JPM expects ABG shares to underperform near term and sees the broader dealer group trading lower until the market gets more clarity on how much of the weakness is DMS-related versus fundamental.

reports.title: F3Q Preview - Capacity Considerations
reports.headline: DB previews MSFT F3Q with likely modest rev upside and Azure strength, but argues AI capacity allocation to apps/security/internal R&D may cap near-term Azure upside and keep focus on proving AI monetization ex-Azure.
reports.summary: Deutsche Bank previews MSFT F3Q ahead of the April 29, 2026 print, expecting another solid qtr with rev around guidance-to-slightly above, Azure likely above the 37-38% cc guide but only modestly because constrained capacity is being allocated across Azure, first-party apps, security, and internal R&D. DB stays constructive on the long-term setup and keeps Buy/$575 PT, but argues the stock increasingly needs tangible AI monetization outside Azure as capex, allocation tradeoffs, and sentiment around competitive AI risk remain key debates.
important_observations: DB sees underlying Azure demand/supply support for growth above guide, but thinks allocation choices likely limit the beat to roughly 1 pt rather than a major upside print.

The central debate is not demand but where scarce AI capacity is deployed; prioritizing first-party apps, security, and internal R&D may sacrifice near-term Azure monetization for longer-term platform value.
DB estimates Azure contributes more than 60% of FY26 incremental rev, reinforcing that Azure remains the primary growth engine even as investors look for broader AI monetization.
The note argues investor burden is shifting toward evidence of AI payoff above the infra layer, especially in M365/Copilot and other application surfaces.
DB views fears of near-term competitive displacement in enterprise productivity/systems of record as overhyped, but acknowledges sentiment risk until usage and monetization data improve.
Commercial bookings could be stronger than expected due to renewals, policy changes around annual consumption thresholds, and customers reserving capacity for rising inference needs.
DB expects capex growth to decelerate materially in FY27 even if absolute spend remains up y/y, implying capacity constraints and allocation tradeoffs may persist beyond this qtr.
new_data: DB models F3Q rev/EPS at $81.2bn/$4.06 vs Street $81.3bn/$4.05.
DB models F4Q rev/EPS at $88.0bn/$4.27 vs Street $88.0bn/$4.25.
The note highlights 15m paid M365 Copilot seats as still early penetration, roughly ~1% of the broader M365 base by DB's framing.
DB cites prior mgmt commentary implying Azure could have grown above 40% if all incremental GPUs had been allocated there, suggesting $2bn+ ARR worth of capacity may be directed elsewhere.
The note points to announced M365 price increases effective July 1, 2026, including Office 365 E3 to $26 from $23 and Microsoft 365 E3 to $39 from $36.
DB cites recent product/strategy items including Agent 365 GA and a new E7 SKU starting May 1 as potential future ARPU tailwinds.
DB references commercial cRPO growth accelerating to 39% y/y and commercial RPO reaching $625bn as evidence of strong forward demand.
management_insights: Mgmt has indicated a relative preference to allocate AI capacity toward first-party apps, security features, and internal R&D rather than maximizing near-term Azure growth.
Last qtr mgmt suggested Azure growth could have exceeded 40% with different GPU allocation, implying deliberate tradeoffs rather than pure supply shortfall.
Mgmt last disclosed 15m paid M365 Copilot seats, supporting traction but still not enough to visibly change M365 Commercial Cloud growth yet.
Prior commentary framed Azure guidance as capacity-driven, and DB believes that characterization likely still holds for F3Q and likely F4Q.
Mgmt has guided F3Q commercial bookings to healthy core growth ex-OpenAI, supported by strong renewal rates.
DB expects mgmt to remain disciplined on op margin despite elevated AI investment and increased R&D focus.
The report flags org changes during F3Q including a unified Copilot organization across commercial and consumer plus a reorganized Copilot leadership team.
evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported historical results, guidance, product disclosures, and prior mgmt commentary form the core evidence base.
Analyst model work: the note is largely a preview built on DB segment forecasts, margin assumptions, and scenario analysis around capacity allocation.
Triangulated: DB supplements company data with IDC/Gartner PC shipment data, Bloomberg consensus, and industry capex/market datapoints.
Channel-check / anecdotal elements are present in bookings, migration, GPU pricing, and server demand commentary, so some conclusions are inferential rather than fully verified.
Overall weight: moderately strong for near-term setup and framing, but several key calls on Azure upside limits and AI monetization timing are still mostly analyst inference.
risks_or_watch_items: Azure upside may remain capped if incremental capacity continues to be diverted to apps, security, and internal AI R&D.
The stock may need clearer proof of AI monetization outside Azure, especially in M365/Copilot, to sustain sentiment.
Capex growth is still very high in FY26, while FY27 may bring slower growth and continued allocation friction.
Memory pricing, component costs, and geopolitical conflict could pressure capex efficiency, supply, and F4Q/FY27 planning.
Commercial bookings strength could still be constrained medium-term if customers want more capacity than MSFT can provision.
MPC remains volatile given PC demand, inventory normalization, Windows refresh timing, and gaming execution.
Potential moves into frontier models could increase compute intensity and crowd out externally monetizable workloads.

reports.headline: MS reiterates OW and $450 PT on CVNA ahead of 1Q26, arguing near-term volatility should be used opportunistically if retail GPU holds and mgmt supports a recovery path.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley previews CVNA's 1Q26 print with a constructive stance, reiterating OW and $450 PT while arguing investor caution is largely about near-term margin execution, macro sensitivity, and retail GPU recovery rather than a broken long-term thesis. The note frames the key debate around whether CVNA can sustain growth and margin progress through temporary reconditioning inefficiencies and fuel/logistics pressure, while also highlighting underappreciated optionality from the company's expanding new-car dealership strategy and longer-term platform uses in parts/service and autonomous fleet management.
important_observations: Investor positioning appears tactically cautious despite long-term bullishness, creating room for upside if 1Q retail GPU and forward commentary are merely solid rather than spectacular.
MS sees the current retail GPU pressure as transitory execution friction from reconditioning buildout, not a structural deterioration in unit economics.
Recent web data suggested unit deceleration exiting the qtr, but channel checks indicated a rebound in recent weeks; that tension is central to the setup into earnings.

The note argues the market may be underappreciating CVNA's emerging platform optionality beyond used autos, especially via new-car dealership acquisitions, parts/service adjacency, and longer-term fleet-management use cases.
MS expects less prescriptive 2Q/FY26 guidance this qtr given macro uncertainty, which could matter for sentiment even if core results are acceptable.
Capital intensity is flagged as a future debate: today's physical model looks unusually asset-light, but recon capacity expansion could require higher capex over time.

reports.summary: Barclays remains Overweight on HCA but argues the path to management's 2026 core EBITDA growth target of 6-9% is getting harder after 1Q26 showed both transient disruption and softer underlying trends. The note highlights weaker employer-sponsored mix, fewer Medicaid conversions leading to higher uninsured volumes, and softer consumer confidence, while acknowledging upside from pending Medicaid state-directed payment programs. Barclays trimmed 2026-27 ests, cut its valuation multiple, and lowered PT to $496 from $551.
important_observations: Implied 1Q26 organic EBITDA growth was only ~3-4% depending on assumed resiliency savings, below the pace needed for the year.
Payor mix deterioration, not just weather/flu, is the more important debate point because it suggests a fundamental trend slowdown.
Potential upside from Florida and Nevada Medicaid SDPs is sizable at $300-600mn+, but Barclays says much of that already appears embedded in buy-side expectations.
To meet Street ests, HCA likely needs about $400mn of resiliency cost savings, manageable ACA exchange headwinds, and a meaningful core earnings reacceleration.
Barclays cut its valuation multiple to 9.5x from 10.25x, reflecting more uncertainty around commercial trends and macro pressure from softer consumer confidence and inflation/geopolitics.

reports.title: F3Q26 Preview: Move CY26 & CY27 EPS Ests to ~$125 and ~$150; PT to $975 (See 6-7x P/E on Peak as Still Appropriate)
reports.headline: Wells lifts SNDK sharply into F3Q26 on stronger NAND pricing, AI/eSSD demand, and higher peak EPS assumptions, but stays EW on valuation.
reports.summary: Wells Fargo sharply raises SNDK ests and PT ahead of F3Q26, arguing it had underappreciated EPS leverage from NAND price inflation and a more disciplined supply backdrop through 2027. The note pushes CY26/CY27 EPS to roughly $125/$150 in the narrative and materially above prior published ests in the model, with the bull case increasingly tied to AI-driven eSSD/KV-cache demand, LTAs, and structurally higher GM. Even so, Wells remains EW because it views the stock as already discounting peak earnings at roughly 6-7x peak EPS, while the key debate shifts toward durability of thru-cycle margins and whether SNDK can convert AI storage demand into sustained eSSD share gains.
important_observations: The central debate has shifted from near-term upside to how much of current earnings power is peak versus sustainable thru-cycle profitability.
Analyst now views NAND vendor supply discipline through 2027 as more credible, supporting a materially stronger pricing and margin framework than previously assumed.
Buy-side focus appears to be moving toward +80% GM and $150+/sh EPS scenarios, versus company-guided 65%-67% GM for F3Q26.
eSSD share remains the main strategic swing factor: low current share is being interpreted by some investors as upside in a supply-constrained market rather than a weakness.
KV-cache/inference storage demand is increasingly treated as a legitimate NAND demand driver, with Wells supportive of SNDK's 2027-2028 opportunity sizing.
Despite large est increases, the analyst still sees upside as capped by a consensus valuation framework anchored to 6-7x peak EPS.

reports.title: New OpenAI Agreement Clarifies Relationship – Cleaner Path Ahead for MSFT
reports.headline: Barclays views the revised OpenAI agreement as a net positive for MSFT because it clarifies economics, removes AGI-related complexity, preserves Azure's product-first position, and reduces MSFT's capital burden from OpenAI infrastructure needs.
reports.summary: Barclays argues the new OpenAI agreement creates a cleaner and more workable structure for both parties and is a net positive for MSFT. The note highlights that MSFT retains revenue share through 2030 regardless of AGI progress, keeps OpenAI IP rights through 2032 on a non-exclusive basis, and benefits from Azure remaining the first cloud for new OpenAI innovation. Barclays also sees strategic value in MSFT no longer needing to fund all of OpenAI's data center buildout, freeing capital for Copilot and broader cloud/GPU capacity while allowing both firms to pursue more independent enterprise AI strategies.
important_observations: The revised structure appears to reduce strategic friction as OpenAI expands across multiple clouds while MSFT develops its own LLM and enterprise AI stack.
Removal of the AGI clause materially simplifies the commercial relationship and lowers the risk that economics would be disrupted by a subjective AGI trigger.
Azure keeping first access to new OpenAI innovation preserves a product and go-to-market edge even as exclusivity eases.
Reduced obligation to support all OpenAI data center demand could improve MSFT capital allocation flexibility toward Copilot and core Azure/GPU capacity.
Barclays frames the agreement as enabling a cleaner separation just as OpenAI's enterprise ambitions increasingly overlap with MSFT's own offerings.
new_data: MSFT receives OpenAI-related revenue share until 2030 independent of OpenAI technology progress.
MSFT maintains OpenAI IP rights until 2032, though no longer on an exclusive basis.
Azure remains the hyperscaler where new OpenAI innovation will be shipped first.

Korea memory export growth and TrendForce forecast hikes both point to a sharper-than-expected memory upcycle, with ASP inflation doing most of the work.
AAPL appears to be taking share in a weak global smartphone market, with scale and memory procurement positioned as a strategic advantage in a constrained DRAM/NAND backdrop.

Cadence’s AI agent launches and deeper Google/NVIDIA ties suggest a possible path to monetization upside beyond its usual growth algorithm if usage-based pricing gains traction.

reports.headline: Agentic AI broadens the AI trade from GPUs to full-stack infra, with CPU orchestration and memory becoming the next major bottlenecks.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley argues agentic AI shifts value creation from standalone accelerators toward system orchestration, lifting CPU, DRAM, packaging, foundry and infra content across the stack. The note frames CPU as the control plane for multi-step AI workflows and estimates agentic workloads can add $32.5-60bn of incremental CPU TAM by 2030 within a $100bn+ server CPU market, alongside 15-45EB of incremental DRAM demand. The firm highlights global beneficiaries across memory, substrates, foundry, BMC/memory interface, storage and semi-cap, and raises PTs on key Korea names tied to memory and ABF exposure.
important_observations: The bottleneck is shifting from raw GPU throughput to orchestration, data movement and memory hierarchy, which broadens AI upside beyond accelerators.
CPU-side orchestration is framed as the dominant latency source in agentic workloads, implying structurally higher cluster CPU intensity and a rising CPU:GPU ratio.
Memory is presented as an active architectural layer rather than passive storage, supporting persistent context, recall and continuous learning.
Morgan Stanley prefers exposure via enablers where capex/token growth maps more directly into earnings upside, especially memory, foundry, ABF and infrastructure ICs.
Supply-constrained layers such as leading-edge foundry, ABF substrates, BMCs, memory interconnect and equipment are positioned to capture outsized economics as system complexity rises.

reports.title: Oil Flash Note: Simple math
reports.headline: JPM argues the oil market is clearing via forced demand loss and heavy inventory draws after a Hormuz-related supply shock, with further price upside likely needed to balance the market.
reports.summary: J.P. Morgan frames this as a macro commodities flash note rather than single-stock research, arguing that a roughly 14 mbd supply disruption tied to the Strait of Hormuz closure has overwhelmed normal balancing mechanisms because Gulf spare capacity is effectively inaccessible. The note says the market has instead adjusted through massive inventory draws and forced demand loss, especially in the Middle East, Asia, and Africa, with Europe and potentially the US still needing to absorb more of the rebalancing. JPM’s key conclusion is that current prices have not yet done enough work to clear the market globally, implying further tightening and higher prices may be required.
important_observations: What looks like demand destruction is, in JPM's view, largely forced demand loss caused by missing physical supply rather than normal price elasticity.
The usual first shock absorber, Gulf spare capacity, is effectively unavailable, making this disruption structurally harder to offset than a typical supply outage.
The remaining balancing gap appears too large for EM demand weakness alone, implying Europe and the US may still need to absorb more rationing via higher prices.
Petchem feedstocks appear to be the earliest and largest point of adjustment, suggesting downstream industrial weakness could deepen before broader transport fuels fully roll over.
The Americas are relatively insulated near term, but JPM flags early signs that higher pump prices and airfares are beginning to dent US driving and jet demand.
new_data: JPM estimates global supply disruption at 9.1 mbd in March, widening to 13.7 mbd in April.
Observable commercial and strategic inventories drew 4.0 mbd in March and 7.1 mbd in April.
Observed global oil demand fell 2.8 mbd in March and is tracking down 4.3 mbd in April.
Middle East, Asia, and Africa account for 87% of the 4.3 mbd April demand hit.
Feedstock-driven weakness represents about 55% of April demand loss; jet fuel is about 11% of the loss.
Russian supply is said to be down 350 kbd in recent weeks, while Russia's spare capacity is only about 300 kbd.
US shale response is limited near term, with only 0.3-0.7 mbd likely over 3-6 months and up to 1 mbd over 6-12 months.
India March LPG consumption fell 13% YoY, cited as evidence of feedstock/fuel stress.
evidence_quality: Company-reported/public market data: draws on Kpler, IEA, EIA, JODI, Singapore, PAJ, Bloomberg, and Rystad; evidence base is largely market/industry data rather than company-specific disclosures.
Analyst model work: core conclusions rely on JPM's balance math and inferred global demand-loss estimates; a meaningful portion is analyst synthesis rather than directly observed data.
Primary vs inference: inventory data are partly observable, but JPM explicitly notes incomplete visibility, especially in product stocks, so true draws may differ materially.
Triangulated but not fully verifiable in real time: the note combines multiple datasets and market price signals, but several conclusions depend on interpreting shortages and residual balancing gaps.
No mgmt meeting/call, channel checks, or survey evidence is cited; weight should be placed on macro balance analysis, with caution around fast-moving disruption assumptions.
risks_or_watch_items: Further price upside may be needed if Europe and the US must participate more fully in demand rationing.
Inventory visibility is incomplete, especially for product stocks, creating risk that actual draws are either larger or less sustainable than observed.
Jet demand could weaken further in May as airlines across Asia and Europe cut activity.
Diesel and jet markets in Europe already appear tight; additional refinery constraints could broaden product stress into gasoline during the US summer driving season.
A slower-than-expected non-Gulf supply response, especially from US shale, would keep the market tighter for longer.
Regional dislocations in Asia and Africa could intensify if cargoes continue to be diverted toward higher-bidding markets.
key_numbers: Supply disruption: 9.1 mbd in March; 13.7 mbd in April.
Observable inventory draws: 4.0 mbd in March; 7.1 mbd in April.
Demand loss: 2.8 mbd in March; 4.3 mbd in April.
Residual balancing need: about 2 mbd even assuming an aggressive 8 mbd inventory contribution.
Regional concentration: 87% of April demand hit in Middle East, Asia, and Africa.
Feedstocks share of April demand loss: about 55%; jet fuel share: 11%.
Brent futures avg: just under $100/bbl in March and April to date; dated crude avg: $107 in March and $123 in April to date.
US shale response: 0.3-0.7 mbd over 3-6 months; up to 1.0 mbd over 6-12 months.
Russia spare capacity: about 300 kbd; recent Russian supply decline: 350 kbd.

reports.title: Oil Flash Note: Simple math
reports.headline: Hormuz disruption has removed enough supply that further price-led demand rationing likely needs to spread beyond EM, with Europe and potentially the US next.
reports.summary: J.P. Morgan argues the oil market is not clearing through normal shock absorbers because Hormuz-related disruptions have effectively stranded Gulf spare capacity. With global supply losses estimated at 9.1 mbd in March and 13.7 mbd in April, the market has instead rebalanced via unusually large inventory draws and forced demand loss, especially in the Middle East, Asia, and Africa. The note’s main implication is that the current demand response likely is still insufficient relative to the scale of missing barrels, so additional adjustment may require higher prices and broader demand weakness, including in Europe and eventually the US.
important_observations: The key variant view is that observed demand weakness is not classic price-driven destruction but forced consumption loss caused by unavailable physical supply.
Saudi/UAE spare capacity, normally the first balancing lever, was effectively cut off from global markets, removing the system’s main shock absorber.
Even assuming aggressive inventory draws, JPM still sees a residual balancing gap that emerging markets alone are unlikely to absorb.
Europe appears next in line for tightening given diesel and jet exposure, while the Americas are only relatively insulated in the near term.
Petchem feedstocks are absorbing the largest part of the adjustment, implying downstream industrial disruption rather than just consumer fuel pain.
Gasoline has lagged distillates so far, but JPM expects that insulation to fade as refinery constraints spread into broader product balances.
new_data: Estimated global supply disruption of 9.1 mbd in March, widening to 13.7 mbd in April.
Estimated observable commercial and strategic inventory draws of 4.0 mbd in March and 7.1 mbd in April.
Estimated global oil demand loss of 2.8 mbd in March and 4.3 mbd so far in April.
Middle East, Asia, and Africa account for 87% of the April demand hit.
Feedstock-related weakness represents about 55% of the April demand loss; jet fuel is 11% of the loss.
Russian supply is down about 350 kbd in recent weeks; Russia’s spare capacity is estimated at roughly 300 kbd.
US shale response is framed as only 0.3-0.7 mbd over three to six months, with up to 1 mbd only over six to 12 months.
India March LPG consumption fell 13% YoY, cited as evidence of feedstock/fuel stress.
evidence_quality: Primary market/industry balance work from J.P. Morgan Commodities Research using Kpler, IEA, EIA, JODI, OilChem, PAJ, Singapore, Rystad, and Bloomberg data.
Evidence is mostly market/industry data plus analyst model work; no company-reported results or mgmt meeting/call content.
Inventory and demand estimates are partly inferred and not fully observable, especially for product stocks, so precision should be treated with caution.
The framework is triangulated across multiple datasets, but the conclusion about required further price adjustment is still materially analyst inference.
risks_or_watch_items: Whether any Hormuz disruption eases before broader OECD demand rationing is required.
Visibility into true product inventory draws remains incomplete and could change the apparent size of the balancing gap.
Potential for sharper diesel/jet tightening in Europe and spillover into US summer gasoline balances.
Speed and magnitude of US shale response may be too slow to offset near-term disruption.
Further airline cutbacks and petchem operating-rate reductions could deepen non-OECD demand loss.
Additional Russian infrastructure attacks could worsen supply tightness beyond the note’s base arithmetic.
key_numbers: Supply disruption: 9.1 mbd in March; 13.7 mbd in April.
Inventory draws: 4.0 mbd in March; 7.1 mbd in April.
Demand loss: 2.8 mbd in March; 4.3 mbd in April.
Residual market-clearing gap cited at roughly 2 mbd even assuming aggressive inventory support.
Brent futures averaged just under $100/bbl in March and April-to-date.
Dated crude averaged $107/bbl in March and $123/bbl in April-to-date.
US shale response: 0.3-0.7 mbd over 3-6 months; up to 1 mbd over 6-12 months.
Russia spare capacity: about 300 kbd; recent Russian supply decline: about 350 kbd.
Feedstocks drive about 55% of April demand loss; jet fuel about 11%.
India LPG consumption: -13% YoY in March.

reports.title: Blackstone Group (BX): Slowdown in Credit fee growth and Real Estate remain headwinds to BX’s growth algo; reiterate Neutral
reports.headline: Goldman Sachs cut BX DE/EPS estimates and lowered its price target to $116, arguing muted management-fee growth from Real Estate weakness and slower Credit fee growth will keep shares range-bound despite stronger PE, Infra, Secondaries, and institutional Credit trends.
reports.summary: Following 1Q26 results, Goldman Sachs reiterates Neutral on Blackstone and lowers 2026-2028 distributable earnings/EPS estimates plus its 12-month price target to $116 from $118. The core thesis is that Real Estate and BCRED, which together represent more than 40% of management fees, should remain near-term drags on fee-related earnings growth, while stronger fundraising and activation in Private Equity, Infrastructure, Secondaries, and institutional Credit are not enough to offset slower fee conversion, retail credit outflows, and softer real estate performance/fundraising timing.
important_observations: Real Estate and BCRED are expected to be the main drags on 2026 fee-related earnings growth because they comprise more than 40% of management fees and are both under pressure.
Goldman sees BX's underlying FRE growth algorithm at only 6%-7% in 2026, with a sharper rebound pushed into 2027 rather than near term.
Institutional Credit remains a bright spot, but mix shift toward lower-fee insurance/institutional capital and BCRED outflows reduces the earnings conversion from AUM growth.
Real Estate fundraising risk is rising because recent BREP vintages show weak returns and realizations are outpacing fundraising, potentially capping the size of the next flagship vintage.
The stock still screens expensive versus peers at roughly 25x NTM P/E and 30x implied P/FRE, limiting upside until growth visibly re-accelerates.
new_data: 12-month price target reduced to $116 from $118; target FRE multiple cut to 26x from 27x.
Goldman now expects only 6%-7% management fee growth in 2026, with acceleration to 11%-12% in 2027 and 2028.
1Q26 total base management fee growth was 8% YoY; Real Estate management fees were down 3% YoY.
BCRED had 1Q net outflows of $1.4bn.
Institutional Credit/insurance fundraising was strong, with about $40bn of Credit fundraising in 1Q and $34bn of inflows referenced for institutional credit momentum.
IG private credit platform reached $130bn, up 23% YoY.
Credit segment available capital was $74bn, with about 85% expected to generate incremental management fees upon deployment.
management_insights: Management suggested YoY management-fee growth should decelerate until later in 2026.
Private Equity fee conversion is delayed by fee holidays, with fuller fee-rate contribution skewed to 2H26.
Management characterized April fundraising weakness in BXINFRA and BXPE as episodic and tied to macro volatility and the Iran war, not structural.
Management said institutional demand from pensions, sovereign wealth, and insurance clients remains robust, especially in IG private credit and ABF.
Management indicated BCRED demand remains subdued despite roughly flat April flows, implying continued near-term retail credit headwinds.

estimate_change_details.note: DE per share estimate lowered after 1Q26 results.
DE per share estimate lowered on weaker fee-growth outlook.
DE per share estimate lowered on slower medium-term growth expectations.
PT cut as Goldman rolled forward Q5-Q8 FRE estimates and reduced target FRE multiple to 26x from 27x.

evidence_quality: Primary evidence: based on company-reported 1Q26 results and management commentary from the earnings release/call.
Primary evidence: includes company-reported segment data, fundraising, fee growth, AUM/FPAUM, performance, and flow datapoints.
Analyst model work is significant: estimate cuts, FRE growth framing, SOTP valuation, and multiple compression are Goldman inference layered on top of reported data.
Some forward-looking judgments rely on management guidance and analyst assumptions about fundraising timing, fee holidays, deployment, and retail flow trends rather than hard backlog conversion.
risks_or_watch_items: Watch BCRED flows through end-2026 and possibly early 2027; continued outflows could offset institutional Credit strength.
Track fundraising and performance for next Real Estate flagship vintages, especially BREP IX and other T-2 vintages.
Monitor timing of fee activation after PE fundraising and fee holidays, particularly BCP Asia III, SP X, and Energy V.
Follow whether April Wealth-channel softness in BXINFRA and BXPE rebounds as management expects.
Valuation remains a risk if growth stays muted for longer than expected.
key_numbers: Neutral rating; price target $116 vs prior $118.
Shares closed at $122.33 on 2026-04-23, implying about 5% downside to PT.
Real Estate management fees in 1Q26: -3% YoY.
Institutional Credit inflows in 1Q: $34bn; total Credit fundraising about $40bn.
IG private credit platform AUM: $130bn, up 23% YoY.
Credit available capital: $74bn; about 85% expected to earn fees when deployed.
BREP IX had returned about 50% of committed capital with a 5% net IRR as of 2026-03-31.
Goldman models next RE flagship vintage at about $27bn, flat v. prior vintage.
Stock valuation cited at about 25x NTM P/E and 30x implied P/FRE.

reports.title: Hermès International SCA: Expert Call Feedback: Resale Demand Softens
reports.headline: UBS expert-call feedback points to softer Hermès resale demand, especially outside core icons, while maintaining a Neutral rating and €1,765 price target.
reports.summary: UBS frames a more cautious read-through for Hermès from a follow-up expert call with a curated luxury resale platform founder: secondhand demand has softened over the past six months, sell-through is taking longer, and non-core models are under pressure, although store-fresh bags and Birkin demand remain relatively resilient. The note suggests weakening resale support could matter for primary demand and indicates rising VIP preference for rarity, special editions, and exotics, while broader soft-luxury resale trends also look mixed to weaker. UBS keeps a Neutral rating and €1,765 price target, with forecasts implying modest 2026 earnings pressure before reacceleration in 2027.
important_observations: Resale demand for Hermès bags has weakened over the last six months, with a growing mismatch between asking prices and consumer appetite.
Weakness is concentrated in non-core models such as Evelyne, Picotin, and Lindy; Birkin is described as the only bag model with clearly resilient demand.
Within quota bags, preference has shifted materially toward Birkin versus Kelly, implying the market is rewarding the strongest iconography and brand-code scarcity.
Store-fresh bags can still clear, but time-to-sell has lengthened and pre-loved inventory faces greater pricing pressure because supply is higher.
VIP demand appears to be migrating toward rare colors, novelties, unique specifications, and exotics, reinforcing a bifurcated market between true scarcity and more available product.
Improved access in markets such as China, helped by lower pre-spend requirements, may be reducing perceived scarcity for historically rarer models.
Steep resale discounts outside bags, including RTW and accessories, point to weaker broader brand monetization in secondary channels.
The expert's tone on Hermès turned less constructive, which matters because resale-market strength has historically supported primary-market desirability.
new_data: Expert call conducted on 2026-04-17 with founder of an ultra-curated luxury resale platform.
Expert says overall secondhand Hermès bag demand has softened over the past six months.
Birkin/Kelly preference within quota bags has shifted to roughly 70/30 from about 50/50 previously.
Sourcing of Hermès bags is about 70% from professional resellers and has become easier outside rare models.
RTW resale discounts are around 70% to retail.
Shoes and accessories such as belts are trading at roughly 70-80% discounts to retail.
China access to Hermès bags has improved due to lower pre-spend requirements, according to the expert.
estimate_changes: No explicit estimate revision versus prior UBS forecasts disclosed in the note.
UBS maintains Neutral rating and €1,765 price target.
UBS forecasts imply 2026 revenue growth of 4.2%, EBIT down 0.1%, and EPS down 1.1%, followed by stronger 2027 recovery.

estimate_change_details.note: Price target history in the report shows a cut from €1,820 on 2026-03-30 to €1,765 on 2026-04-16.

evidence_quality: Primary/near-primary: UBS cites company accounts, LSEG Eikon, and UBS model forecasts for financials and valuation.
Channel check / expert call: key incremental thesis rests on a follow-up call with the founder of a curated resale platform, which is informative but anecdotal and sentiment-sensitive.
Triangulated only partially: resale observations are detailed within handbags but broader comments on jewelry and other brands are explicitly noted by the expert as less certain.
Mostly analyst inference for stock impact: the bearish read-through to primary demand and brand health is UBS interpretation built on secondary-market evidence rather than company guidance.
risks_or_watch_items: Further softening in resale demand could weaken the scarcity halo that supports full-price primary demand.
Non-core model pressure and longer sell-through times may be an early warning of broader normalization in luxury demand.
If access continues to improve in China and other markets, perceived exclusivity could erode for less-rare Hermès products.
VIP concentration in ultra-rare product may narrow the brand's demand breadth and make performance more dependent on top-end clients.
UBS highlights broader macro/discretionary spending slowdown, brand-image deterioration, raw-material volatility, and supply-chain disruption as key risks.
key_numbers: Birkin/Kelly preference about 70/30 vs about 50/50 previously

reports.title: Volumes Were Iced Out in Q1, Impacting Margins, But SDP/Resiliency Theses Hold
reports.headline: Jefferies keeps Buy on HCA after a weather- and respiratory-driven Q1 EBITDA miss, cuts PT to $525 from $590, and argues SDP approvals, resiliency savings, and strong FCF preserve the bull case.
reports.summary: Jefferies views HCA’s Q1 miss as largely transitory: winter storms and a weak respiratory season pressured same-store admissions and created fixed-cost deleverage, but revenue was roughly in line, FY2026 revenue/EBITDA/EPS guidance was reaffirmed, SDP expectations were raised, and the firm believes underlying demand, AI/resiliency-driven cost savings, pending state-directed payment approvals, and robust free cash flow keep the medium-term thesis intact despite a lower price target.
important_observations: Underlying EBITDA growth was framed as stronger than reported once adjusting for weather, respiratory weakness, and supplemental payment timing, with Jefferies citing normalized EBITDA growth of 3.5%.
The Q1 miss appears more margin/deleverage-driven than demand-collapse-driven; same-store revenue per equivalent admission still rose 3.1%, suggesting pricing/acuity held up better than volumes.
Incremental SDP upside remains a key debate point, with Jefferies expecting further approvals in Florida and Virginia after positive Georgia and Texas developments.
Management’s resiliency/AI program is positioned as a meaningful offset to policy and reimbursement pressure, with Jefferies pointing to a $400MM EBITDA contribution this year.
Free cash flow strength remains central to the thesis because it supports both growth CapEx and continued buybacks even through uneven quarterly operating results.
Variant perception may hinge on whether investors treat Q1 as a one-time weather/flu distortion or as an early sign of softer utilization and weaker commercial mix.
new_data: Q1 2026 revenue was $19.1B, roughly in line with Street expectations.
Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA was $3.73B, 1.9% below Street.
Same-store admissions rose 0.9%; same-store adjusted admissions rose 1.3%; same-store revenue per adjusted admission rose 3.1%.
Weather reduced admissions by about 30 bps and weak respiratory trends by about 70 bps in the quarter.
HCA recognized $200MM of SDP in Q1 versus prior expectations of $80MM, helped by Georgia DPP and reinstatement of the Texas ATLIS program.
HIX impact was estimated at $150MM in Q1.
FY2026 guidance was reaffirmed at revenue $76.5B-$80.0B, EBITDA $15.55B-$16.45B, and EPS $29.10-$31.50.
FY2026 SDP guidance was revised upward by $200MM at the midpoint to a range of negative $50MM to negative $250MM.
Q1 free cash flow was $704MM.
management_insights: Management reaffirmed FY2026 revenue, EBITDA, and EPS guidance despite the Q1 miss, signaling confidence that the weakness was temporary.
Management increased SDP expectations for FY2026, reflecting improved outlook from state-directed payment developments.
The resiliency program remains a major operating lever, and Jefferies says management expects it to contribute about $400MM of EBITDA this year.
Management appears focused on using strong cash generation to fund CapEx and share repurchases while preserving growth.

estimate_change_details.note: Prior inferred from the report's stated +3% change to Jefferies FY2026 EPS estimate; exact prior value not printed.
Jefferies lowered PT while maintaining Buy, citing 10.5x FY2027 EBITDA.

evidence_quality: Primary: based on company-reported Q1 2026 results and reaffirmed FY2026 guidance; high relevance for quarterly read-through.
Primary plus analyst model work: Jefferies combines reported metrics with its own variance analysis, normalization bridge, and revised forecasts.
Partly triangulated but still inference-heavy on drivers such as weather, respiratory weakness, SDP timing, and resiliency offsets; medium confidence on normalized earnings power.
Forward-looking upside from pending SDP approvals and additional cost savings is mostly analyst inference rather than confirmed company guidance; lower confidence than reported results.
risks_or_watch_items: Pending Florida and Virginia SDP approvals are important swing factors for upside.
eAPTC expiration and broader Medicaid/policy changes could pressure EBITDA if offsets fall short.
Commercial mix softness, lower acuity, or prolonged volume weakness would challenge the thesis that Q1 was transitory.
Site-neutrality policy risk remains a sector overhang.
Resiliency/AI savings may ramp slower than expected.
A recession could reduce higher-margin commercial procedures.

reports.title: In Any Hand That Is Dealt, GE Holds All the Cards
reports.headline: Jefferies stays Buy on GE, trims PT to $365 from $375, and argues conservative guidance leaves upside in services and 2028 targets.
reports.summary: Jefferies frames GE Aerospace as a high-visibility aerospace compounder whose conservative 2026 guidance understates services upside, backlog strength, and the runway to exceed 2028 targets. The note highlights stronger-than-guided commercial services momentum, resilient OE and defense demand, improving free-cash-flow capacity, and a path to 2028 operating profit above management’s $11.5B target, while acknowledging macro risk from higher oil prices, air-travel sensitivity, tariffs, and GE9X/LEAP execution.
important_observations: Services is the central variant view: Jefferies sees material upside to 2026 commercial services despite management’s cautious tone and flat-to-low-single-digit departures assumption.
The 2028 framework appears intentionally conservative, with Jefferies estimating $12.9B of 2028 operating profit versus management’s $11.5B target.
Q1 services strength looks structurally supported by backlog, delinquent parts, and already-scheduled shop visits rather than just a one-quarter pull-forward.
Narrowbody aftermarket, especially LEAP and CFM56, is the main earnings engine; Jefferies expects LEAP services to become a much larger profit contributor as warranty and durability issues normalize.
Widebody services also improved, helped by higher GE90/GEnx workscopes and slower-than-expected retirements, extending the revenue tail.
GE9X remains an earnings drag and an important offset to otherwise strong volume and aftermarket dynamics.
new_data: GE reaffirmed 2026 guidance but reduced its departures forecast to flat-to-low-single digits from mid-single digits.
Management now assumes Brent rises in Q2/Q3 to roughly $105/bbl before easing back by Q4.
Q1 2026 commercial services grew 39% y/y; internal shop visits rose 35% and spare parts were implied up 45%.
Services orders rose 49% y/y in Q1, including more than 30% growth since early March.
Services RPO reached $171B, up about $30B over five quarters and about 22% versus YE24.
Spare-parts delinquencies are up 70% versus YE24, reinforcing the supply-demand imbalance.
GE raised its 2026 services growth expectation by $500M to $4.0B y/y from $3.5B.
Q1 engine deliveries rose 43% y/y; LEAP deliveries were up 63% to 520 units.
DPT Q1 orders rose 67% y/y to $6.2B; DPT RPO increased to $30B from $25.9B at YE25.
management_insights: Management took an unusually cautious tone, emphasizing macro and oil-price uncertainty and signaling scar tissue from past downturns.
GE reaffirmed 2026 guidance rather than raising it despite strong Q1 execution.
Management expects Q2 2026 services growth in the high teens.
Two-thirds of the internal shop visits embedded in guidance are already off-wing, suggesting good near-term visibility.
Management expects tariff headwinds to be mitigated through cost actions, pricing surcharges, duty drawbacks, USMCA compliance, exemptions, foreign-trade zones, and rerouting work.
Management continues to guide to 2028 sales growth of double digits, operating profit of $11.5B, and roughly 100% FCF conversion.

estimate_change_details.note: Jefferies raised 2026 adjusted EPS to $7.50 from $7.45.
Jefferies raised 2027 adjusted EPS to $8.65 from $8.55.
PT cut to $365 from $375 while maintaining Buy.
2026 revenue estimate change shown as less than 1%, but prior Jefferies revenue estimate is not disclosed in the excerpt.
2027 revenue estimate change shown as less than 1%, but prior Jefferies revenue estimate is not disclosed in the excerpt.
Jefferies 2028 adjusted EBIT estimate is $12.9B, presented against company target rather than a prior Jefferies estimate.

evidence_quality: Primary company-reported evidence: Q1 results, reaffirmed 2026 guidance, backlog/RPO, order data, and disclosed operational metrics carry high weight.
Management call/meeting interpretation is important here; the cautious tone and oil/departures assumptions are direct but still partly qualitative.
Analyst model work is substantial: long-dated revenue, services, margin, FCF, and valuation bridges are mostly Jefferies inference rather than newly disclosed company commitments.
Evidence is triangulated with company data plus industry/market inputs such as fleet growth, airframe production, and Cirium data, but the 2027-2030 upside case remains forecast-dependent.
Some conclusions rely on extrapolating current services demand, pricing, and workscope trends, so confidence is strongest near term and lower on outer-year margin expansion.
risks_or_watch_items: Higher oil prices and the knock-on effect on global air travel and departures.
Whether Q2/Q3 traffic and utilization hold up well enough to support the services growth implied in Jefferies’ model.
LEAP durability restoration, warranty unwind, and pace of margin improvement.
GE9X negative engine margin, launch learning curve, and timing of 777X entry into service.
Tariff and cost-inflation offsets versus planned pricing and productivity actions.
Supply-chain constraints, spare-parts shortages, and whether delinquency/backlog converts cleanly into revenue.
Defense order conversion and mix in DPT.
Capital deployment pace, especially buybacks and dividends versus FCF and leverage.
key_numbers: Buy rating maintained; PT cut to $365 from $375.
Q1 2026 commercial services +39% y/y; Q2 services expected high teens.
2026 services growth expectation raised to +$4.0B y/y from +$3.5B.
Services RPO $171B; up about $30B over five quarters.
Q1 services orders +49% y/y; spare-parts delinquencies +70% versus YE24.
Q1 engine deliveries +43% y/y; LEAP deliveries +63% to 520.
2026E adjusted operating profit $10.26B; 2028E adjusted operating profit $12.9B versus company target $11.5B.

reports.title: Remain Underweight following the Q1 setback
reports.headline: Morgan Stanley keeps Underweight on HCA after a Q1 miss, arguing most of the volume shortfall was temporary but upside to FY26 remains limited absent better supplemental payments.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley maintains its Underweight rating and $425 price target on HCA after a weak Q1 driven by a soft respiratory season and winter storms, which the firm estimates hurt EBITDA by about $180 million. While management said volumes improved in February and March and reaffirmed core 2026 operating targets, Morgan Stanley still sees limited upside to guidance outside supplemental-payment approvals, with exchange disruption and future policy risk remaining the key swing factors. Annual 2026-2028 estimates and the price target are unchanged, though some quarterly revenue and EBITDA estimates were adjusted.
important_observations: The Q1 miss appears more timing-related than structural, but Morgan Stanley still does not see enough earnings upside to justify a better stance on the stock.
Supplemental payments were the main offset in Q1 and remain the biggest near-term source of upside if Florida approvals come through.
Exchange disruption is tracking within prior assumptions, but the shift toward uninsured and lower-quality exchange mix remains a meaningful drag on collections and EBITDA.
Morgan Stanley argues Tenet may be set up better than HCA into earnings because HCA's respiratory weakness may be less relevant there and Tenet's estimates look more conservative.
Peak valuation leaves little room for operational or policy setbacks as investors move closer to potential 2027-2028 healthcare policy headwinds.
new_data: Q1 same-facility equivalent admissions rose 1.3% y/y.
Respiratory weakness reduced quarterly admissions growth by 70 bps and ER visit growth by 140 bps.
Winter storms reduced admissions and ER visits by about 30 bps and 50 bps, respectively.
Morgan Stanley estimates respiratory and storm impacts together hurt Q1 EBITDA by about $180 million.
Supplemental payments benefited Q1 by about $200 million versus Morgan Stanley's $80 million expectation.
Exchange disruption reduced Q1 EBITDA by about $150 million; management still expects a $600-$900 million FY26 EBITDA headwind.
Exchange admissions fell about 15% in Q1; uninsured admissions rose 16%, with more than half tied to exchange shifts.
Management now expects the FY supplemental-payment benefit decline to be $50-$250 million versus the prior $250-$450 million decline.
management_insights: Management said February and March volumes rebounded after January weather and respiratory weakness.
Management reaffirmed 2%-3% equivalent-admissions growth for Q2-Q4 and EBITDA margins slightly above 20% for 2026.
Management remains constructive on Florida supplemental-payment approval, which could provide upside to current guidance.
Management said exchange-related volume loss was at the low end of the prior 15%-20% assumption.
Management noted some shift from Silver to Bronze exchange plans, though not yet significant.
Management highlighted AI rollout areas including ambient listening, nurse handoff, patient safety, and nurse engagement tools.

estimate_change_details.note: Raised after reported Q1 results.
Modest upward revision to quarterly forecast.
Cut to reflect Q1 shortfall from respiratory and weather effects.
Raised, likely reflecting recovery timing and supplemental-payment assumptions.
Full-year EBITDA estimate unchanged.
Price target unchanged.

evidence_quality: Company-reported Q1 results and guidance update; primary evidence and highest-weight input in the note.
Management commentary from the earnings release/call on volumes, exchanges, and supplemental payments; primary but management-framed.
Analyst model work versus prior forecasts and consensus; useful for framing revisions but partly inference-driven for out-quarters.
No channel checks, survey work, or proprietary dataset evidence presented here; external triangulation appears limited.
risks_or_watch_items: Florida supplemental-payment approval and any other state program approvals.
Whether Q2-Q4 admissions actually return to the 2%-3% growth target.
Magnitude of FY26 exchange-market disruption versus the $600-$900 million EBITDA headwind assumption.
Further migration from exchange to uninsured or lower-value Bronze plans, which could pressure collections.
2027-2028 policy risk around ACA subsidies, Medicaid funding, and reimbursement.
Potential reemergence of wage pressure or weaker utilization trends.
